summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a significant energy affordability crisis in 2025–2026, with electric bills for the state's five largest investor-owned utilities rising an average of 17.5% in a single year — with NIPSCO customers seeing the highest spike at 27% and CenterPoint customers at 25%. In response, Governor Braun signed HEA 1002 in February 2026 — requiring multi-year rate plans, new summer disconnection protections, and mandated low-income assistance programs — and the IURC launched an unprecedented 'investigative inquiry' and statewide listening tour. Despite these reforms, consumer advocates and the Office of Utility Consumer Counselor remain sharply critical of recent rate orders, including the IURC's June 2026 approval of a $71 million rate increase for AES Indiana and ongoing allegations that Duke Energy has over-collected $89 million from ratepayers.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households on fixed incomes
messaging_recommendations.message: Rising utility bills are not inevitable — Indiana law now requires utilities to offer you assistance programs and protections from shutoffs on the hottest days. Know your rights: if you are enrolled in LIHEAP/EAP, your utility cannot disconnect you during extreme heat or winter months. Apply for the Energy Assistance Program when it reopens in fall 2026.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and IURC commissioners
messaging_recommendations.message: HEA 1002 is a first step, but Hoosiers are still seeing rate increases that disproportionately burden residential customers over industrial users. Strong, consistent enforcement of affordability metrics in multi-year rate plans — and full accountability for utilities found to over-collect — is essential to make the new law meaningful.

Indiana's utility bills have risen more sharply in the past year than at any point in two decades, and the pending AI data center build-out poses an additional threat to residential ratepayers who could be left subsidizing massive industrial energy loads. Community pressure — through IURC listening tours, public comment periods, and direct contact with lawmakers — is producing real results.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Community action agencies and social service providers
messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana's LIHEAP-funded Energy Assistance Program provides a critical safety net for eligible households, but federal funding threats and program gaps mean that millions of Hoosiers — including those served by municipal utilities not covered by IURC jurisdiction — may fall through the cracks. Proactive enrollment outreach and coordination with utilities on payment plans is more urgent than ever.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a ~$491.5 million annual revenue increase in two phases. The IURC approved approximately $295.7 million in additional annual revenues in January 2025, roughly 60% of what was requested; a compliance appeal by the OUCC filed June 2026 alleges Duke over-collected $89 million under the approved order.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$295.7 million annual revenue increase approved (approx. +$19.16/month for 1,000 kWh residential customers); OUCC alleges additional $89M over-collection under appeal

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed for a regulatory rate review in June 2025 seeking up to $293 million in increased revenues; a settlement agreement was filed October 2025 and modified by the IURC. The IURC issued a final order on June 16–17, 2026 approving approximately $71 million in additional annual revenues, to be implemented in two phases starting July 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$71 million annual revenue increase approved; less than $5/month per phase for typical 1,000 kWh residential customer

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed for a major electric base rate increase in September 2024. A settlement agreement was reached in early 2025 and the IURC approved it in June 2025, phasing in rate increases beginning July 2025 through early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~16.75% overall increase approved; average residential customer (672 kWh/month) sees ~$23/month increase; phased implementation July 2025 through early 2026

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy Indiana's electric utility in southwestern Indiana sought an $118.8 million base rate increase in three phases. The IURC approved the increase on February 3, 2025, with the third phase taking effect in March 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$80–$118 million revenue increase approved; average residential customer (799 kWh/month) bills rising approximately 20%, with final phase taking effect March 2026

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Gas billing investigation
rate_cases.description: The IURC opened a formal investigation after unsealed records revealed NIPSCO overcharged more than 3,500 natural gas customers — billing them double their actual usage — following a new metering technology rollout. NIPSCO discovered the error in October 2024 but did not report it to the IURC until November 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Affected customers received credits equal to 50% of gas usage charges for 12 months prior to discovery; no amounts sought from underbilled customers

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana received IURC approval for a temporary fuel adjustment charge increase for summer 2026, citing higher-than-predicted fuel costs during Winter Storm Fern in January 2026. The OUCC did not oppose the increase.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$3.98/month for 1,000 kWh customers; in effect June–August 2026

articles.headline: State regulators approve $70M increase for AES, less than utility originally sought
articles.summary: The IURC voted 3-1 on June 17, 2026 to approve a $71 million electricity rate increase for AES Indiana customers, significantly less than the $293 million AES originally sought. Phase one rates take effect July 2026 and phase two in January 2027; both the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition condemned the order, with OUCC Counselor Abby Gray calling it 'an outrage.'

articles.headline: State's ratepayer advocate accuses Duke of overcharging customers in appeal to regulators
articles.summary: Indiana's OUCC filed a formal appeal in June 2026 accusing Duke Energy Indiana of over-collecting more than $89 million from ratepayers under a rate order that was later corrected by presiding officers, and of being on track to over-collect another $86 million annually. Both the OUCC and the Citizens Action Coalition, along with several industrial ratepayers, are pursuing the appeal, which could take several months to resolve.

articles.headline: Indiana now blocks utility shutoffs on the hottest days — but only for some customers
articles.summary: A new Indiana law (HEA 1002) blocks privately and cooperatively owned electric utilities from disconnecting service within 48 hours of a forecast heat index of 95°F or higher — but only for customers enrolled in federal energy assistance (LIHEAP). Consumer advocates welcomed the protection as a meaningful first step while noting that a broader time-based rule would have covered more vulnerable Hoosiers.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers will see a temporary increase in bills this summer
articles.summary: AES Indiana customers face an additional $3.98/month fuel adjustment charge from June through August 2026, due to higher-than-predicted fuel costs during Winter Storm Fern in January 2026. The IURC approved the temporary increase with no opposition from the OUCC; the charge applies on top of a base rate increase approved in June 2026.

articles.headline: How Indy's rising electricity bills stack up
articles.summary: An Axios analysis found that Indianapolis-area electricity bills have surged significantly, with AES Indiana seeking a 13.5% increase on top of prior increases. The article tracks how bills compare across Indiana's five investor-owned utilities and examines the OUCC's ongoing affordability investigation.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: At a March 24, 2026 IURC investigative inquiry, Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities defended their rate increases before regulators and the OUCC, with utility representatives arguing usage patterns and infrastructure costs drove higher bills. IURC Chair Andy Zay was visibly unsatisfied with the responses, and the IURC announced a 10-session statewide listening tour to hear directly from ratepayers.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Indiana Public Media
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay announced on February 25, 2026 that the commission would conduct a formal investigative inquiry into the affordability practices of Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities — an unprecedented move for the typically understated regulatory body. Zay said the balance between utility profitability and customer affordability feels 'dramatically out of whack' and cited a surge of customer complaints.

articles.headline: NIPSCO admits to overcharging more than 3,500 customers after new metering technology rollout
articles.summary: Newly unsealed records show NIPSCO overcharged more than 3,500 natural gas customers — billing them double their actual usage — following a new metering system rollout, and did not notify the IURC for nearly a year after discovering the issue in October 2024. NIPSCO began issuing credits covering 50% of gas usage charges for 12 months, while the IURC opened a formal investigation and lawmakers called on the attorney general to probe the company.

articles.headline: Gov. Braun says new law will protect Indiana utility customers from rising bills
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun held a ceremonial signing of HEA 1002, Indiana's major utility reform law, pledging to personally enforce its affordability and accountability provisions. The law introduces multi-year rate plans tied to performance metrics, mandates low-income assistance programs, levelized billing, and summer shutoff protections; some consumer advocates said the law does not go far enough.

articles.headline: Utility affordability push is worthy endeavor even if some customers are left out
articles.summary: An analysis notes that Indiana's electric bills jumped 17.5% in one year on average, and consumer advocates warn that roughly one in five Hoosiers served by municipal or cooperative utilities remain outside IURC jurisdiction and thus outside the scope of new affordability reforms. The piece examines the scope and limits of HEA 1002 and calls for broader protections.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair, Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission
stakeholders.quote: "That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Indiana Public Radio, IURC affordability inquiry news conference

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair, Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission
stakeholders.quote: "In my short time as Chairman of the IURC, my fellow Commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills. These listening sessions are an opportunity for us to meet with people in their local communities." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: The Republic / Indiana Public Media, IURC affordability listening tour announcement

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The AES rate order issued today is an outrage and Hoosiers deserve better! Governor Braun has made it clear that ratepayer affordability is a priority, far more than just a 'hot topic' as described by the chairman of the IURC today. This order fails the governor's call to overhaul how utilities are regulated." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / WISH-TV, statement on IURC AES Indiana rate order

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "Any case before the Commission sets a precedent that could be relied on in future cases." (exact quote, regarding the OUCC's appeal of Duke Energy's over-collection)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, statement on Duke Energy overcollection appeal

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "This was a very, very good thing for the state of Indiana. We and others have for years been working towards getting similar disconnection moratoriums for the high-heat days." (exact quote, on Indiana's new summer shutoff protections under HEA 1002)
stakeholders.source: WFYI / WBOI / WVPE, reporting on Indiana's new summer utility shutoff law

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Conservation Voters / CAC joint press release, Ratepayer Relief Plan

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "a profoundly unbalanced arrangement that benefits AES Indiana and the narrow interests of a small handful of some of its largest electricity users." (exact quote, on AES Indiana rate settlement; CAC later joined a revised settlement)
stakeholders.source: Axios Indianapolis, AES rate case coverage

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "For more than a decade, AES Indiana has maintained some of the lowest rates in the state through disciplined planning and a focus on efficiency. We applied the same approach here by working closely with stakeholders to make balanced decisions that keep the system reliable, limit customer impact, and position the company for future investments." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Fox59, statement on IURC approval of AES rate increase

stakeholders.name: Megan Robertson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosier families are stretched thin, and they need relief now from monopoly utilities who've pushed policies for years that led to this crisis. This plan gives state leaders a clear, practical path to lower bills that will protect Hoosiers most in need and build a more affordable, reliable energy future for every community in our state." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Conservation Voters / CAC joint press release, Ratepayer Relief Plan

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor of Indiana
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is the top kitchen table concern for Hoosier families. I appointed a new majority to the IURC and a new consumer advocate to take on high energy costs and ensure utilities provide service at just and reasonable rates." (exact quote, posted on social media during March 24, 2026 IURC inquiry)
stakeholders.source: Governor Braun Twitter/X post, cited in Fox59 and WNDU coverage of IURC inquiry

stakeholders.name: J.D. Ford
stakeholders.title: State Senator, District 31
stakeholders.quote: "It really goes to show you that Hoosiers were speaking out about affordability issues and that we were listening. Did the bill do everything I wanted it to do? No, but it's definitely a step in the right direction." (exact quote, on HEA 1002)
stakeholders.source: WFYI, reporting on HEA 1002 ceremonial signing
